[2:21:45] Absolutely. Should you close with a slide deck? Yes or no? Where possible, I think you should. Okay?

 

[2:22:00] Are all slide decks created equally? The framework that we showed you, is this the be-all, end-all, hard and only way to create a sales or a pitch closing slide deck?

 

[2:22:18] No. This is the start of your journey. If you're not aware of slide decks, this is just a very, very high-level tip of the tip of the iceberg in terms of slide decks.

 

[2:22:35] This is not a slide deck presentation. We're not going to go into that. We just want to make you aware. Then from...

 

[2:22:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Procurement Process - RFP Stage"

 

NÚMEROS: 8 steps total in procurement

 

VISUAL: Processo de procurement B2B, RFP (Request for Proposal), tender documents

 

[2:22:50] You know the weather, there is a proposal or a tender in place, and you receive an email from a company saying, hey, here's a tender document, and you need to submit it by tomorrow. What does that tell you?

 

[2:23:08] I'll give it to you. It tells you that you're being shopped around. It probably tells you that they've already made their choice on the vendor, but because they need a third vendor as part of the procurement process, they contact you.

 

[2:23:28] Now, you can strategically use that. I mean, if your company, if you have leeway and autonomy to lower your price or even go in and go, we're not going to turn this into a negotiation, but, you know, that's a key, again, that was a very advanced thing that what Gael just mentioned to you.

 

[2:23:48] When those who had experience, you know that you've been shopped around and you're probably already losing the bid because literally they just, you're just a number. They just need you in there to get through the SOP, the rule of they need three.

 

[2:24:08] And then those rules, by the way, are created normally in larger companies, you know, publicly traded companies, you know, enterprise-sized companies. And the reason they do that is to mitigate the monopoly, you know, and so, and of course, you know, to drive down costs, right?

 

[2:24:28] So, you know, they retain the control that when they have, you know, they shop, you know going into a deal that you're going to have to bid and enforce yourself to be on the low bid or, you know, to be the one is many times.

 

[2:24:48] But then there's also some politics involved as well too, right? You got a hero, you got the people that can help you out. You know, you might actually still win the tender if you've hit all the other things as well. So, Gael, continue.

 

[2:25:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "RFP Strategy - Draft with Sales"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estratégia de trabalhar com sales para rascunhar RFP a seu favor

 

[2:25:08] Yeah, so you're going to be aware of that. And on the other hand, how do you use this to your advantage? Sometimes, and I've done it in my own experience, you want to work with your sales to actually draft the RFP with them in favor of you.

 

[2:25:28] So if you manage to do that, then you know that you have good chances of winning. But if you're being shopped around at the end of as a third vendor or the fifth vendor, you know, you're not going to win.

 

[2:25:45] So, the lesson learned is get early in your account. And if there is no formal proposal, RFP in place or standard document in place, work with your sales to define the decision criteria so that the tender is drafted in your favor.

 

[2:26:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pro Tip - Proactive RFP Assistance"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Dica profissional: oferecer ajuda proativa para criar RFP

 

[2:26:08] Here's a pro tip, okay? You are in touch with, you know, you've identified who your sales is. And the sales might be a department head that really likes you. You know, they want it because it's going to make their life easier, right?

 

[2:26:28] Remember the three levels, the onion? You know, from a company standpoint, from the departmental standpoint, they're definitely, for the department, they're definitely for the individual, okay?

 

[2:26:45] So they have a vested interest, but maybe the CFO, the ruler, other people, even the, you know, the operations, right? You know, the explorer, they may not. They don't know. They're cold. They don't know you. They don't communicate.

 

[2:27:05] So work with your sales. Say, I want to help you. And pro tip is actually, once you know this, this demonstrates that you're a B2B closer.

 

[2:27:22] When you are, you know, if you go in, can you imagine now you're in a B2B situation and you've done the closing, but if you did not know that these are the steps, you're a rookie, then you would just sit there and wait and hope that your sales tells you, oh, by the way, you know, we need to put in a tender.

 

[2:27:45] Versus when you are closing, because you're always closing. During the meeting, you say, by the way, John, you know, I mean, I'm pretty sure your company has some form of, you know, RFP, right? Some sort of a, you know, request for proposals, a tender, you know, what if I could actually help you?

 

[2:28:08] Why don't we work through that together? Let me help you to win this deal. Let's help to win and get the approval from finance and procurement. How does that sound?

 

[2:28:25] Many times, and of course, this comes from relationship building. And that's why sometimes B2B sales, you know, people go play golf or they do business trips, these kinds of things, because they're nurturing their relationship so that when you get to that point, sometimes even mentioning this could happen over a dinner.

 

[2:28:48] You know, I mean, I'm talking now we're in the way the pandemic is, people are not really meeting face to face, but many times, don't be shy to ask this. This is a critical step that most AEs fail to do.

 

[2:29:05] If you want to move the needle forward, ask, just ask for it. And many times they'll say, yeah, actually, yeah, you're right. We, you know, we do have to, and maybe your stage doesn't like to do it.

 

[2:29:22] So you said, don't worry about it. You know, I don't know your exact company styles, but, you know, I've done it. I've helped my other clients to do it. I'll make it easy for you. Let's, one, send me your RFP and I'll go over it together with you. I'll help you to fill it out, you know, and there'll be a higher likelihood you get approved for the budget.

 

[2:29:45] What do you think they say? I'm trying to say it right. Sorry, I'm trying to say it right. If you're going to an RFP, chances are that the guy who wrote the RFP is the one who's going to win the deal.

 

[2:30:02] That's so true. Bingo, bingo.

 

[2:30:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Purchase Requisition (PR) Stage"

 

NÚMEROS: Step in procurement process

 

VISUAL: PR (Purchase Requisition) necessária antes de compra, blanket PR concept

 

[2:30:10] Then procurement needs to raise a purchase requisition. So you've got to be aware of this because if the deal stalls, it might be because the purchase requisition, a PR, is not raised.

 

[2:30:28] So you want to work with the procurement and ask them, okay, is there a PR raised already for this purchase? Because if there is no PR, they cannot buy. And that's a step that procurement has to do.

 

[2:30:48] So the reason why I'm laying out those steps is for you to work with the client and help them navigating through this. So you want to ask, okay, is there a PR in place? How much is the PR amount?

 

[2:31:05] You may hear sometimes, you may have sometimes a blanket PR. That's also something that is used. The blanket PR could be, for example, a purchase request for the whole year.

 

[2:31:22] So let's say procurement would say, for all of my IT needs this year, I will raise a purchase request for $10 million. And this way it will be easier for procurement and finance to raise purchase orders and provision it. Because this step takes time.

 

[2:31:45] So you want to ask, have you raised the PR already?

 

[2:31:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Purchase Order (PO) - Most Important Document"

 

NÚMEROS: PO number required for payment

 

VISUAL: PO como documento legal vinculativo, detalhes incluídos, número de PO

 

[2:31:52] Then it moves to the most important. That's a document that you want, the purchase order. The purchase order is also raised by procurement. It's a legal formal document that binds the customer into paying you with your amount.

 

[2:32:12] So in the PO you will have delivery details, supplier, customer, the different SKUs, the price, tax, payment terms. In a PO there is always a PO number at the top. And that's what you need to get paid, basically.

 

[2:32:32] That's important because that's what you're going to need to put in your invoice. Keep in mind...

 

[2:32:40] Go ahead, Kyle. Yes, sorry. I was just going to finish on that. In my case, in tech or in all my deals, that's a document I need in order to book my deals.

 

[2:32:55] So I'm not getting compensated or I'm not getting credited for anything unless I have a purchase order in. That's right. As an AE, that's one of your KPIs. That's how you get paid.

 

[2:33:12] You're compensated on your variables when you submit on your side. On your company's side, you have the purchase order. Is that correct? Yeah.

 

[2:33:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Procurement Process - Multiple P's"

 

NÚMEROS: RFP, PO, PR mentioned

 

VISUAL: Múltiplos "P's" no processo: RFP, PO, PR, importância de entender cada um

 

[2:33:30] So keep in mind... I was going to say, I think I kind of lost my thought for a second there. But keep in mind, you can see there's a lot of P's here, right? RFP, PO, right? You know, you've got all the different steps here.

 

[2:33:50] Not everything looks identical to this. Again, what we're trying to demonstrate is understand the process. Now, obviously, if you're working in an enterprise company and you're being trained by a sales manager, they will normally take you under their wing. They'll apprentice you many times, you know.

 

[2:34:12] Sometimes if you're hired, you want to demonstrate that you've got some experience. This is a great way to do that. Even if you're going for a job interview, okay, hypothetically, let's say you're going for a job interview, these are great questions to ask, not just to the clients once you get a job, but into the job interviewer.

 

[2:34:35] You're asking them questions, you know, what sort of slide decks do you guys have, a pitch closing deck do you use? You know, for the average clients you're dealing with right now, what have we noticed in terms of sales cycles?

 

[2:34:52] That's demonstrating the business acumen. You know, do most of the, you know, does our company require, you know, purchase orders? Do we require purchase orders from our clients, you know, from their procurement?

 

[2:35:12] All these questions demonstrates that you are now, you know, you may have zero B2B experience. You may actually be going on Indeed and actually applying for something.

 

[2:35:28] Now, I'm not saying fake it till you make it. I'm not trying to say be, you know, the catch me if you can guy. But what I'm saying is at least you now are equipped. Is this making sense? Is this helpful for those who have no experience?

 

[2:35:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Vendor Registration (VRF) - Step 7"

 

NÚMEROS: Step 7 (but happens earlier)

 

VISUAL: Registro de vendor necessário antes de compra, due diligence legal

 

[2:35:50] It comes at number seven, but that's actually something that you should do early on is obviously registering yourself, your company as a vendor.

 

[2:36:08] Companies cannot make a purchase if you're not listed as a vendor in their procurement system. So you want to work with the procurement officer early on by registering your company in their system so they can actually pay you.

 

[2:36:28] They do this for legal and compliance reasons. They do due diligence on your company, make sure that you don't have anything that's going to affect them. We don't have the time to go into all of this.

 

[2:36:45] Yeah, even though it's step seven here, sometimes it happens at an earlier step, even step four, step three, you know, where you definitely have to register. It's basically vendor registration. It's a VRF.

 

[2:37:02] You'll only see it. It'll usually come from either the accounting team or legal team will say, you know, procurement team will say, you know, we need you to fill up a VRF, right?

 

[2:37:18] Because they legally cannot proceed forward if you're not registered with them. And sometimes they do that because maybe you're working for a competitor, right?

 

[2:37:32] So, you know, they need to know that you've been vetted. And there's many, again, people in the company that don't know if you are not. So for other departments, they will go and check the vendor registration to see if you have been vetted. If you are, then okay, we'll continue the conversation with you.

 

[2:37:52] Does that make sense? And then finally.

 

[2:37:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Finance - Invoice Approval & Payment"

 

NÚMEROS: Final step

 

VISUAL: Etapa final: aprovação de invoice e pagamento pela finance

 

[2:38:00] Finally, finance. Invoice approval and payment. That's why you have the PO and you have all the details. Finance can pay. So finance needs all of those details in order to raise, in order to pay for the invoice.

 

[2:38:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Stakeholder Mapping Strategy"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Importância de mapear todos stakeholders (finance, legal, procurement) cedo

 

[2:38:22] So now that you know all the stakeholders, it's important because now you know that you can start mapping all the finance, legal, procurement offices early so that you can start getting the legal terms and conditions approved early on by the legal department.

 

[2:38:45] You can start the procurement registration process with the procurement and finance team early on so that you can avoid any delays.

 

[2:39:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pro Tip - Don't Piss Off Procurement/Legal"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Aviso crítico sobre importância de ser profissional com procurement e legal

 

[2:39:02] Pro tip for you here, okay? This is usually where the CFO does the final sign off of the controller. Sometimes even CEO, but many times it's not even CEO. Okay?

 

[2:39:20] Pro tip here. If you piss off procurement, if you piss off legal, I can guarantee you, you will not get through this stage. So be nice. Even if they're asking you painstakingly to fill out ridiculous bureaucratic documents or things and they're taking forever, be nice to them.

 

[2:39:45] No bribery, none of that stuff, just be nice. Be professional through all the stages. Make their lives easier. At the end of the day, many of the time the people in these companies, they just have a job. That's their role. They take pride in it. That's their things they're good at. They're detail-oriented people.

 

[2:40:08] If you're not a detail-oriented person, make sure you have someone on your team who is, who can help you out. An executive assistant, something like that.

 

[2:40:22] I've seen AEs who are, especially sales, typical type people tend to be very outwardly extrovert-ish. They're good with the face-to-face interactions or communications. But when it comes time to filling out documents, statements of work, all those kind of things, they really suck at it.

 

[2:40:45] So many times you'll have, in sales teams, you'll have assistants who actually help, who actually kind of bridge that gap, and then they help to communicate on your behalf.

 

[2:41:02] If they like you, I can guarantee you when it gets to the finance team, finance will ask, finally, what's been your interaction with? And if these detailed people, they pay attention, you're late for a meeting, they requested something, but you responded slowly to them, you submitted a document, but there were mistakes in it.

 

[2:41:28] I can guarantee you all those add up. Finance team is taking note of that, and they may even just delay the payment. Okay, we've seen it happen before. You do not want that to happen at this stage. Okay, pro tip there. Moving on.

 

[2:41:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Payment Terms - Accounts Payable/Receivable"

 

NÚMEROS: 30, 60, 90 days payment terms, 6 months mentioned

 

VISUAL: Termos de pagamento, cash flow management, AR/AP concept

 

[2:41:50] Yeah, and it's a good question from Ivan. Yes, in B2B, the product gets delivered before the payment simply because in the terms and conditions, the payment terms could be 30 days, 60 days, 90 days forward, right?

 

[2:42:12] So you want to deliver first, and then that's what the PO is for. The PO is a promise of purchase. So you know that if you had a PO, the customer is going to pay for it, but it will take time for finance to process it.

 

[2:42:32] They're doing that for cash flow. They want to keep as much cash as possible. And this is what sometimes hurts the cash flow of your company, right? Because those become accounts receivable that are outstanding, they haven't been paid yet, right?

 

[2:42:52] 60-day terms, 90-day terms. You know, sometimes we've even seen where it's, what is it, six months, right? Six-month terms before the actual money comes in.

 

[2:43:08] So you need to, and they're checking to see whether you deliver. Okay, what is this one about?

 

[2:43:18] Exactly, that's the concept of account payable and account receivable by the finance department. So it will take time. And that's something that is negotiated by the legal or the finance department. So that's why you want to start this process early on.

 

[2:43:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Procurement Process Summary"

[2:43:40] So that's it for the B2B procurement process. I hope it was helpful for you to understand the different departments, and more importantly for you to start early by communicating with each stakeholder and addressing each step so that you know exactly where the deal is.

[2:56:58] But I really can't knock down the fees for you. But what I can do for you instead, maybe I could give you additional deliverables on top of the 10 we have already discussed.

 

[2:57:18] I can also provide additional training for your staff. So they will all have a thorough understanding about the project and I can also ensure that they will implement the initiatives.

[2:57:58] I don't know. I believe what I propose is a pretty fair deal already, right? And, you know, I mean, can you be more flexible since this is our first collaboration?

[3:01:55] You know, with the, and we're using the word Sage, right? So just so you guys know. But actually, we wouldn't say that, right? So I've seen your proposal, you know, and already discussed the plans with John.

[3:02:35] I mean, is there any way you can knock down the fees, you know, maybe say, what, 30%? You know, that way I can then propose it to, you know, CFO and help to kickstart this immediately.

[3:03:22] Well, you know, I mean, the reason I asked for the 30% is because, I mean, it will help me to convince the CFO, right? I mean, even in my role, I have authority, but for, you know, a purchase order like this, you know, we definitely need to get finances approval, you know, and our CFO will need to get involved to move forward with the project.

 

[3:03:50] And also put, you know, given the potential ROI and positive impact, right, you know, on our market share, which you presented, right? Now, although we already have the budget set, we do have some continuity in place, you know, maybe for unplanned initiatives.

 

[3:04:15] Especially since, you know, I mean, the way you presented it, this project comes with a more measurable ROI. You know, is there anything you can do to make that 30%?

 

[3:04:35] Hmm, I can see that you are really keen on getting this project approved. Let me help you here. Since this is already our fourth meeting, you have spoken with John, and here's what I can do.

 

[3:04:58] If I am able to knock down the fees as you proposed, understanding that you do see the value and ROI in this project, and you will ensure that we work through and see the success for this project, does that mean we will be doing business after your meeting with them?

 

[3:05:25] Well, you know, I mean, as long as you agree to the terms I've set, I mean, if you can get that, you know, the 30%, I don't see any problem in getting the approval to move the deal forward and to get the approval from the CFO.

[3:07:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High EQ Importance in B2B"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Importância de EQ alto além de business acumen, lidar com seres humanos

[3:08:20] So why do you think it's important to have high EQ? Let's see what they say.

 

[3:08:32] Better flow control. What else? Build rapport. Trust, empathy, emotional control. Better understanding. More connected. To pick up the non-verbal cues. Yes.

 

[3:08:52] I'm halfway through my Emotion 2.0, but better rapport, definitely. Yes. Correct. So because regardless of whether the deal size is in the 10 of thousands or in the millions, we are dealing with human beings.

[3:13:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Financial Instruments Overview"

 

NÚMEROS: $10 million minimum, up to billions, $50 billion SBLC deal, 3-6 months to 1 year cycle

 

VISUAL: Explicação de financial instruments, SBLC (Standby Letter of Credit), buyer/seller contracts

 

[3:13:58] So and the reason why I hustle my way to earn this, thank you. How many of you I would like to know are in the financial space or even have some knowledge about it? Anyone in the financial space?

 

[3:14:20] Okay, Bobby, yes. I'm not surprised, Bobby. Bobby, you know that you and Pansy are very similar in your culture index as well too. So maybe you guys are very good at problem solving. No surprise, you guys are dealing with finance as well. Okay, keep going.

 

[3:14:42] Okay, so just in a nutshell, for those who are not in the financial instruments or financial industry, financial instruments, monetary contracts that monetize SBLC.

 

[3:15:00] So SBLC basically stands for standby letter of credit. So it is a major financial transaction of assets between two parties. We normally call them the buyer and the seller.

 

[3:15:20] So they are usually representative of large companies that deal with commodities like oil and natural gas. Now, the oil that we are talking about, we are not talking about the olive oil or the cooking oil we use at home. It is jet oil and diesel oil for industrial purpose.

 

[3:15:45] Yeah, crude oil, LNG, right? All those kind of... So the sales cycle for such deals, right, usually range between a minimum of 10 million. It will not be a million. 10 million to the billions.

 

[3:16:08] I did layers with a deal that is looking at 50 billion for SBLC. So because of the size of these deals, the commission payout is also very good.

 

[3:16:28] I remember earlier, I think Guy or Desmond shared about, you know, such big deals, you just need to close one a year or two a year, you can just relax. You can buy a lot of things, you can settle a lot of things.

 

[3:16:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Financial Deal Cycle & Stakeholders"

 

NÚMEROS: 3-6 months to 1 year cycle, 5 levels of stakeholders, CD Bank, Bank of America

 

VISUAL: Ciclo de vendas longo, múltiplos stakeholders incluindo bancos offshore

 

[3:16:50] So for a deal to bottom line, the process usually takes about three to six months to a year as it does not only involve the five level of stakeholders. I think by now you all know who are the five level of stakeholders.

 

[3:17:12] It also involves especially the external authority, which is usually the major offshore banks, the CD Bank and the Bank of America.

[3:17:52] Obtain accurate data of the deals. Ensure that we are able to match the specs. Ensure that the party, maybe the buyer or the seller, depends, is experienced in this business with proven track records and testimonials.

 

[3:18:15] So more often than not, as an advisor, I have to save this deal from human oversight in the negotiation process. And this has helped my client from being scammed or fleek.

 

[3:18:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NDA vs NCNDA"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Diferença entre NDA e NCNDA (Non-Convention Non-Disclosure Agreement)

 

[3:18:40] I will give you an example of what you mean by human oversight that can cost you a big fortune. So I know most of us would know what is NDA. Put it in the chat. Have you signed an NDA before or are you aware what is an NDA?

 

[3:19:02] Okay, so this question is not for Bobby. Tell me, do you know what is a NCNDA?

 

[3:19:20] Non-compete, close, very close. NCNDA, non-disclosure, that's right, NDA. NCNDA stands for non-convention and non-disclosure agreement.

 

[3:19:40] So basically for non-convention is when we are dealing with the buyer and seller, the buyer is not allowed to actually send another third party to contact the seller and bypassed.

 

[3:20:00] So that's why it is important that we sign the NCNDA. And usually this, comparing to the other business deals, this is usually done right in the beginning of the agreement or in the beginning of the deals when we are able to match the specs.

[3:20:52] And usually we don't look at all the fine print, we just sign. Yeah, it's okay because I'm guilty of it also. But in this financial industry, there is no room for error. And you know what? That can also result in major legal damages with lawsuit if you are not careful.

[3:25:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CFO Mindset - ROI Focus"

 

NÚMEROS: ROI = Return on Investment

 

VISUAL: Mentalidade do CFO: tudo sobre ROI, dinheiro, alternativas

 

[3:25:30] And that's why going back a few slides, I was telling you, you want to be definitely, definitely see that even procurement, all these different stages you go through, make friends with everyone in there. Okay.

 

[3:25:52] Now, when it comes to CFOs, everything is about, you know, an ROI. We've covered this. I don't think I need to educate you. ROI is a return on investment.

 

[3:26:12] CFOs are only concerned with return on investment, right? You know, it starts off with a core issue, right, which is always money. So, you know, and then how much money, how much more money will it take, you know, to make the purchase, you know, of your solution, right? This is what's going through the mind of a CFO.

 

[3:26:38] And almost by definition, you know, the ROI will have to be better than other alternatives. Okay, so what are other alternatives? Don't just think ROI as in transactional, like you bring a solution, oh, we're going to give you this new CRM, for example, or we provide you this new software, we provide you this new coaching for, you know, the ROI has to be calculated.

 

[3:27:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CFO Resource Management"

[3:27:35] So sometimes you're not even competing against a competitor. Sometimes you're competing against another initiative altogether. I'll just pause here for a second. Think about that, digest that, okay?

 

[3:27:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CapEx vs OpEx - Capitalize vs Expense"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Diferença entre capitalizar (CapEx) e expensear (OpEx), impacto em balance sheet

 

[3:28:00] And sometimes products and services both have ROI, right? You know, so what is the total ownership costs? The total ownership costs for the products. Basically, in other words, you know, what does the product cost above the initial cost?

 

[3:28:25] Okay, is your solution something that could be capitalized? In other words, you know, paid for all at once or is it expensed, right? You've heard Gael, you know, we talked about this, right, Gael? You know, CapEx, OpEx, right?

 

[3:28:48] Capitalize, expensed, paid for when, you know, expense means, you know, it's paid for when the expenses actually occur. So sometimes, say for example, you know, you're providing a training and, you know, do they pay upfront for the 12-month training or do they pay every month once you've completed the training, right?

 

[3:29:15] That helps to manage and mitigate for cash flow management, for balance sheets. That's what CFOs are interested in, okay?

 

[3:29:32] So capitalizing lower profits at the start, expensing lower profits for period, each period, you know, that the expense occurs. Again, sometimes maybe they're going through different sales cycles. Maybe they're going through different periods.

 

[3:29:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Quarter-End Payment Terms Strategy"

[3:29:58] If it's not been a good quarter, they may not want to be doing expense during that time. They may want to delay and they may want to, when you're dealing with procurement as well, to, you know, different payment terms where it might be 90 days.

 

[3:30:22] Why 90 days? Because it's the next quarter. So then they can expense it during the next quarter. Is that making sense? So, you know, oh, go ahead.

 

[3:30:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "TCO - Total Cost of Ownership"

[3:30:45] Yes? No, I'm just going to say it's commonly known as the TCO, right? The total cost of ownership. And in tech, a lot of solutions have roughly a three-year lifetime expectancy.

 

[3:31:05] So it's common for CFOs to look at a three-year total cost of ownership because they know that in three years, they probably will need to do a refresh of their system. So they look at it from a three-year perspective, yeah.

 

[3:31:28] Yes. Okay, so, you know, we don't have enough time to go into all of this. I just want to give you a bird's eye view. I want to pique your interest.

[3:34:45] But eventually, if you are, I can guarantee you one fast way to make friends with a CFO is you actually take them out for lunch. You offer them to pay for their lunch, right?

 

[3:35:05] Because they don't want to be, I hate to say they're not cheapskates, but don't say, hey, I will take you out for lunch and let's expense it to the company. No, don't do that. That's a surefire way to piss off your own CFO.

 

[3:35:28] Say, I'll pay out of my own pocket. And then during that lunch, when you speak to the CFO, tell them you'd like to learn about, you know, what are the criterias of a CFO? What's important for a CFO, right?

 

[3:35:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Internal Finance Team Consultation"

[3:36:15] But I can guarantee you, you'll get in the door faster if you study and consult with your own CFO, your own finance team internally.

 

[3:36:35] And many times in the B2B world, many sales teams will have meetings with the finance team because the finance team will provide sometimes some consultation to look at the deal and they will make an assessment.

 

[3:36:58] And they'll give the sales team, you know, their perspective on what the client's finance team is going to be likely asking, right? What are the, you know, for risk assessments, you know, and they will help you to understand and navigate those waters.

 

[3:37:22] So when I say the CFO is your friend, it doesn't always mean that it's your own internal finance team. Take them out, let them know the language that's going to make the CFO's ears perk up is, you know, do you mind if I take you out for lunch? I'll pay for it.

 

[3:37:48] Not just that part, but do you mind if I ask you because we're trying to close, you know, $100,000 deal, $200,000 deal, and I'm getting to the stage where I now have to deal with their finance, their CFO.

 

[3:38:12] What, if you were in that position, what are the critical things that we need to get this deal over the finish line? And if we can do this, it's going to bring in revenue to our company. CFO is going to love you for that, okay? Your CFO is going to love you for that.

[3:40:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Speaking CFO Language - ROI Example"

 

NÚMEROS: $3,000 (current spend), $10,000 (ROI), 3.5x ROI

 

VISUAL: Exemplo de como falar a linguagem do CFO com ROI concreto

 

[3:40:38] So that's a substantial improvement when you're putting flyers out. And you're a digital marketing agency. And you said, how much are you spending on ads? Oh, we're spending $1,000 on billboards and $2,000 on sending out letters.

 

[3:41:00] Okay, so that's about $3,000. And how much ROI are you getting out of that? And they say, well, you know, we're actually not seeing much ROI.

 

[3:41:20] I'm not asking you to come up with new budget. I'm asking you, why don't you just shift that $3,000 over to pay us at the agency and even conservatively, our agency is able to bring you an extra $10,000.

 

[3:41:42] So it's about just 3.5 ROI. Would that make sense? Now you're speaking a CFO's language. Okay, now that's overly simplified, right, when you're dealing with a real estate agent.

 

[3:42:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Understanding Company Operations"

[3:42:28] Get to know your explorer, the hero, the sage, the explorer is going to help you to find out. So that way you have cards to play. The CFO will actually be very pleasantly surprised and actually impressed that you actually understand that's what's going through their mind.

 

[3:42:52] Now you may not know all the details, but you can ask. And many times you'll see a CFO, they'll stop and think about it. They may not get back to you, but they're definitely thinking about it, right? Making sense? Yes? Okay, next one.

 

[3:43:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CFO Questions to Ask"

 

NÚMEROS: 10% of marketing spend mentioned

 

VISUAL: Perguntas estratégicas para fazer ao CFO sobre gastos e prioridades

 

[3:43:20] Ask them how much they spend on marketing, how much they spend on legal, how much they spend on most, you know, basically in their P&L. Then ask the CFO if they will consider what you propose, your economic benefits for the product services that you're trying to close.

 

[3:43:48] In other words, the CFO is constantly playing with these weighing scales in their mind. And they're constantly weighing out which one do I put the resources to, which will give me a faster ROI for the company.

 

[3:44:12] And they're calculating multiple variables to figure this out. So as long as you're, it's never so transactional. Sometimes it is, but most of the time it's not. You have to respect that.

 

[3:44:35] And if you respect that, they're having to juggle and weigh out multiple scales. And it's not just like left and right. Think about like a scale that's like six, seven different scales. They're managing that, okay?

 

[3:44:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Value Proposition Examples"

 

NÚMEROS: 10% of marketing spend, people costs = highest costs

 

VISUAL: Exemplos de proposições de valor para CFO: % de budget, savings, productivity gains

 

[3:45:00] Tell them, hey, look, it's only 10% of your overall marketing spend, right? With this, you can look at how much legal expenses you'll be saving or the highest costs are the people costs, right?

 

[3:45:25] So our training program, and I'm just giving you a scenario here, our training program will largely improve the confidence, the productivity, the efficiency of your people.

 

[3:45:48] So imagine how much additional revenue margins of profit it will get you, right? Look, we have proven testimonials to support their improvement and productivity, yada, yada, yada, right? Et cetera, et cetera, et cetera.

 

[3:46:12] Again, come with facts, testimonials. This is why that slide deck, right? When you're getting to that level, you now wanna definitely show the proof. We've mapped it out. Show best case scenarios, worst case scenarios.

 

[3:46:35] They'll be looking at the worst case scenarios because that'll help you to close.

 

[3:46:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Challenge - Find and Role Play with CFO/CPA"

 

NÚMEROS: 1 month, 2 months, 6 months, 1 year timeframes mentioned

 

VISUAL: Desafio para comunidade: encontrar CFO/CPA e fazer role play

 

[3:46:50] Now, we're not gonna do a role play here because we've almost gone for six hours here, right? I think you've all got a lot of value. In fact, if anything at all, I'm gonna challenge you, okay? This is gonna be really ballsy.

 

[3:47:15] How many of you actually are in communication with some sort of an accountant? Or if not, you know how to eventually, you know what I mean? Don't go on LinkedIn and suddenly start bugging accountants.

 

[3:47:38] I would say start off with accountants, right? Try to find, and this is not something that you're gonna do overnight. It might take some of you one month, two months, even six months.

 

[3:48:00] Just like if you went through HTC, some of you, it took a while to get your first boom. It could even be a year from now. The challenge is this. I'm gonna challenge you to get in touch with a CFO.

 

[3:48:25] It doesn't have to be a CFO. It could be a controller as well, even just a chartered accountant or even CPA. And many times they act as a fractional CFO or a temporary part-time CFO.

 

[3:48:48] And okay, Shang says, you know, yes, I were an accountant myself before, so I need some personal conversation with myself. Maybe Shang, you're gonna be like hit up by all the community now. Everyone's gonna be wanting to speak to you.

 

[3:49:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play with CPA/CFO - Next Level"

 

NÚMEROS: 1-10 scale for budget approval

 

VISUAL: Importância de role play com profissionais reais, feedback pragmático

 

[3:49:15] And actually role play. Role play with yourselves. Find someone who actually is, and they're gonna play tough, right? And actually role play this. It's a very different style of closing.

 

[3:49:38] There is no right or wrong way. Get them to give you very pragmatic feedback. On a scale of one to 10, would they approve your budget? Why? You know, at which point did you, there's a tipping scale for them, a tipping point at which they actually approve.

 

[3:50:05] You know, they felt the risk is manageable that you've delivered enough proof to them and built enough trust. This is gonna be very critical for you.

 

[3:50:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mastering CFO Closing = Different World"

 

NÚMEROS: 6, 7, 8 figure deals mentioned

 

VISUAL: Impacto de dominar closing com CFO: acesso a outro nível de deals

 

[3:50:30] This is next level B2B closing. You master this, forget even mastering it. You even cracked the code on this. Just barely cracked the code on this. You have entered a very different world.

 

[3:50:55] It's a skill that I can tell you very few people in the world have. A handful companies pay big bucks for, you know, account executives that can handle this.

 

[3:51:18] And even in your own businesses or even in the, you know, if you're dealing as a service provider, very, very big. It can really make the big difference between six, seven, eight figure deals for you. Yes?

 

[3:51:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Assignments - Sharpen Business Acumen"

 

NÚMEROS: 3 business magazines, 3 companies' financials

 

VISUAL: Tarefas: ler 3 revistas de negócios, 3 financial statements públicos

 

[3:51:45] So with that, let's go into assignments. Finally, assignments. The Emeralds have been looking at the slides on there, like four more to go. We made it. We made it.

 

[3:52:08] So assignment number one, sharpen your business acumen. Read three business magazines for enterprise and SMBs. Which ones? I'm not gonna push you.

 

[3:52:30] You know, Adam, Gael, any others. If you have business magazines that you are reading, discovering, take a screenshot of the cover, post it, or maybe it's online, post it in the Facebook group, right?

 

[3:52:52] You know, if you're reading, if you're reading, and there's several very specific ones. You don't get the super specifics because it can be very boring and dry.

 

[3:53:12] I can tell you if you're brand new at this, don't just start off even reading like Entrepreneur Inc., Financial Times, Bloomberg. Yeah, Bloomberg, Barron's, yeah. WSJ, big time, Wall Street Journal.

 

[3:53:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Handwriting Exercise - Avoid Being a 'Pig'"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Importância de handwrite artigos, mudar sinapses cerebrais, evitar ser "pig" (pretender saber)

 

[3:53:38] All of these will definitely, you know, help to improve your business acumen. If you're a copywriter, well, you know how this works. You read those articles, handwrite some stuff.

 

[3:53:58] When you handwrite, something very critical happens in your brain. The synapses change. You will start to find that your vernacular will start to change.

 

[3:54:20] You don't want to be a pig. In the industry, we call that someone being a pig. A pig is someone who pretends to know. They think they read these publications, right, you know?

 

[3:54:42] And of course, any of you, in the high-income copywriter, we talk about the case study of the Bloomberg offering their subscription, right? They are targeting people, you know, who are trying to be pigs, to try to show off.

 

[3:55:05] In the financial industry, we call them pigs, you know? Oh, yeah, you know, I bought this stock. Oh, yeah, this is doing really well, you know? They really don't know shit. They're just retail, okay?

 

[3:55:28] And then all the institutional, you know, bankers were laughing, right? So don't be a pig. Read, educate yourself, you know, learn the vernacular, learn the vocabulary.

 

[3:55:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Read Public Financial Statements"

 

NÚMEROS: 3 companies

 

VISUAL: Tarefa de ler demonstrações financeiras públicas, será overwhelming no início

 

[3:55:52] Read three companies' public financial statements. It's going to overwhelm you. The first time you read it, you're not going to understand it.

 

[3:56:15] You might want to, this is where, see, most of you won't even do this. I have a CPA, and, you know, I pay him, you know, he's not cheap, but, you know, he's very fair and a very good deal.

 

[3:56:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Fractional CFO Strategy"

 

NÚMEROS: $500-$600/month investment

 

VISUAL: Estratégia de contratar CPA como fractional CFO, investimento mensal

 

[3:56:40] And we have our monthly meetings, and many times we'll go over our financials, we figure everything out, you know, and we're done. Normally we have about a two-hour meeting, and I always tell him, I said, I want to leave the last half hour to talk about other things.

 

[3:57:05] And really what I'm doing is I'm picking his brain. We're talking about businesses, I'm showing him, you know, I'm showing him, you know, financials of other companies, I'm asking him how he would look at it. And so he plays a role of fractional CFO as well.

 

[3:57:30] Now, you know, I don't know if you guys are willing to spend this, but I spend about anywhere from $500 to $600 a month, right? But I get that time.

 

[3:57:52] That's a very, at my level, that's a very, that's a necessary investment on my side, right? I'm not saying you guys all have to go out there and do it. You know, when you're making over six figures, seven figures, then it makes sense, okay?

[3:58:42] This takes time. Just change your environment, right? Those of you in HTC sometimes or anything, you'll hear Sifu talk about, or in dinner with Dan, you'll hear...

 

[3:58:42] This takes time. Just change your environment, right? Those of you in HTC sometimes or anything, you'll hear Sifu talk about, or in dinner with Dan, you'll hear him talk about changing your environment.

 

[3:59:02] You know, if you're always hanging around with people that don't talk business, don't understand business, you're not going to level up. So start changing your environment.

 

[3:59:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Assignment 2 - Practice Discover, Clarify, Quantify"

[3:59:45] You know, implement that. See if you can take that and actually apply it in your world, in your deals. And if you're not doing deals yet, role play it. Role play with your brothers and sisters.

 

[4:00:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Assignment 3 - Create Your Slide Deck"

 

NÚMEROS: 5-step storyline

 

VISUAL: Terceira tarefa: criar slide deck seguindo framework de 5 passos

 

[4:00:08] Assignment number three. Create your own slide deck. Now, I'm not saying you have to do this by next week. This might take you a month, two months, three months.

 

[4:00:28] But start thinking about it. If you were to create a pitch closing deck, not a marketing deck, a pitch closing deck for your business or for the company you work for, what would it look like?

 

[4:00:48] Follow that five-step storyline we showed you. Big change, winners and losers, future pacing, solution as hero, evidence. Start thinking about that framework.

 

[4:01:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Assignment 4 - Map B2B Procurement Process"

 

NÚMEROS: 8 steps

 

VISUAL: Quarta tarefa: mapear processo de procurement para seu cliente/empresa

 

[4:01:10] Assignment number four. Map out the B2B procurement process for your current client or for your company. Go through those eight steps we covered.

 

[4:01:30] RFP, vendor registration, PR, PO, all of those things. Understand where you are in that process. And if you haven't started yet, understand what those steps look like for the company you're targeting.

 

[4:01:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Assignment 5 - Role Play with Accountability Partner"

[4:02:15] Watch it back. Be critical of yourself. What did you do well? What could you improve? Get feedback from your accountability partner. This is how you get better.

 

[4:02:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bonus Assignment - Study Your Industry"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Tarefa bônus: estudar profundamente sua indústria

 

[4:02:38] And bonus assignment. Study your industry. Whatever industry you're in or you're targeting, become an expert in it.

 

[4:02:58] Read the trade publications. Understand the challenges. Know the jargon. Know the trends. Know who the major players are. Know what's changing in that industry.

 

[4:03:18] Because when you can speak intelligently about their industry, you're no longer just a salesperson. You're a trusted advisor. You're a consultant. You're someone they want to do business with.

[4:25:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Common Questions"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Sessão de perguntas e respostas começando

 

[4:25:20] Okay, I'm seeing some questions coming in. Let me address a few of the common ones.

 

[4:25:38] Question 1: "How do I get started if I have no B2B experience?"

 

[4:25:55] Great question. Start with the assignments. Read those business magazines. Study financial statements. Listen to earning calls.

 

[4:26:12] Then, practice the frameworks with role plays. Get comfortable with the language, the concepts, the processes.

 

[4:26:30] And when you're ready, start prospecting. Use the Ultimate 100 list. Use the Kingpin strategy. Start building relationships.

 

[4:26:48] You don't need experience to start. You just need to start to get experience.

 

[4:27:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Enterprise vs SMB"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre começar com enterprise ou SMB

 

[4:27:08] Question 2: "Should I start with enterprise or small business?"

 

[4:27:25] Most of you will start with small business. It's easier to get in. The sales cycles are shorter. You'll get more reps.

 

[4:27:42] But that doesn't mean you can't work towards enterprise. As you build your skills, as you build your confidence, as you build your track record, you can move up.

 

[4:28:00] Start where you can get traction. Start where you can get wins. Then level up.

 

[4:28:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Slide Deck Creation"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre criação de slide decks

 

[4:28:20] Question 3: "Do I need to create my own slide deck or can I use my company's?"

 

[4:28:38] If your company has a good pitch closing deck, use it. But understand it. Know every slide. Know why it's there. Know what story it's telling.

 

[4:28:58] If your company only has a marketing deck, you might need to create your own pitch closing deck. Or work with marketing to create one.

 

[4:29:18] And if you're an independent consultant or closer, yes, you'll need to create your own. Use the framework we showed you.

 

[4:29:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - CFO Relationship Building"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre construir relacionamento com CFO

 

[4:29:40] Question 4: "How do I approach a CFO without seeming pushy or desperate?"

 

[4:29:58] Great question. You approach them with genuine curiosity and respect. You're not trying to sell them anything.

 

[4:30:18] You're trying to learn from them. You're trying to understand their world. You're trying to become better at your job.

 

[4:30:38] Most CFOs will appreciate that. They'll respect that. And they'll be willing to help.

 

[4:30:58] Just be authentic. Be professional. And be respectful of their time.

 

[4:31:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Dealing with Rejection"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre lidar com rejeição

 

[4:31:20] Question 5: "How do I deal with rejection in B2B? It feels more personal than B2C."

 

[4:31:38] It does feel more personal because you're building relationships. You're investing time. You're getting to know these people.

 

[4:31:58] But remember, it's not personal. It's business. Sometimes the timing isn't right. Sometimes the budget isn't there. Sometimes there are internal politics.

 

[4:32:18] Don't take it personally. Learn from it. Ask for feedback. Understand what you could have done differently.

 

[4:32:38] And then move on. There are plenty of other opportunities out there.

 

[4:32:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Time Management"

 

NÚMEROS: 100 leads/month mentioned

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre gerenciar tempo com múltiplos leads

 

[4:33:00] Question 6: "How do I manage my time when dealing with multiple prospects and long sales cycles?"

 

[4:33:18] Great question. This is where systems and automation come in. Remember what Adam talked about?

 

[4:33:38] Use CRM systems. Use email sequences. Use automation tools. Use templates.

 

[4:33:58] But also, prioritize. Not every lead is equal. Focus on the ones that are most likely to close. Focus on the ones with the highest value.

 

[4:34:18] And use the 30-day increments. Plan your touchpoints. Schedule your follow-ups. Be systematic about it.

 

[4:34:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Pricing and Negotiation"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre pricing e negociação

 

[4:34:40] Question 7: "How do I handle pricing objections in B2B?"

 

[4:34:58] First, make sure you've established value before you talk about price. If you've done your job right, price shouldn't be the main objection.

 

[4:35:18] Second, understand that price is often a proxy for other concerns. Maybe they don't see the value. Maybe they don't trust you yet. Maybe they're comparing you to alternatives.

 

[4:35:38] Third, remember Pansy's approach. Don't fold immediately. Ask questions. Understand what's really going on.

 

[4:35:58] And fourth, tie everything back to ROI. Show them how the investment will pay for itself. Show them the cost of not taking action.

 

[4:36:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Industry Knowledge"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre quanto conhecimento de indústria é necessário

 

[4:36:20] Question 8: "How much do I need to know about an industry before I can close in it?"

 

[4:36:38] You need to know enough to be credible. You need to know enough to have intelligent conversations.

 

[4:36:58] But you don't need to know everything. In fact, sometimes not knowing everything is an advantage. You can ask questions. You can be curious. You can learn from them.

 

[4:37:18] The key is to show that you've done your homework. Show that you understand their challenges. Show that you care about their success.

 

[4:37:38] And then be willing to learn. Be willing to listen. Be willing to become an expert in their business.

 

[4:37:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Remote vs In-Person"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre closing remoto vs presencial

 

[4:38:00] Question 9: "Can I close B2B deals remotely or do I need to meet in person?"

 

[4:38:18] Both work. Nick is a perfect example. He's in Greece closing for a law firm in Los Angeles.

 

[4:38:38] The pandemic has made remote closing more acceptable. More normal. More expected even.

 

[4:38:58] But there's still value in meeting in person when possible. Building that face-to-face relationship. Reading body language. Building trust.

 

[4:39:18] So use both. Start remote. Build the relationship. And when it makes sense, when you're closing a big deal, consider meeting in person.

 

[4:39:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Commission Structures"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta sobre estruturas de comissão em B2B

 

[4:39:40] Question 10: "What are typical commission structures for B2B closers?"

 

[4:39:58] It varies widely. It depends on the industry, the company, the deal size, your experience.

 

[4:40:18] In some industries, you might see 5-10% commission. In others, it could be 20-30% or even higher.

 

[4:40:38] Some companies pay base plus commission. Some are straight commission. Some have accelerators when you hit certain targets.

 

[4:40:58] The key is to understand the economics of the deal. Understand what you're worth. Understand what value you're bringing.

 

[4:41:18] And negotiate. Don't just accept the first offer. Negotiate based on the value you're providing.

 

[4:41:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Thoughts - You Have Everything You Need"

 

NÚMEROS: 6 hours of content, 4+ weeks of program

 

VISUAL: Pensamentos finais sobre ter tudo necessário para sucesso

 

[4:41:40] Alright, I think we've covered the main questions. I'm sure there are more, but we've been going for over six hours now.

 

[4:42:00] And I want to leave you with this. You have everything you need to succeed. You really do.

 

[4:42:20] You have the frameworks. You have the strategies. You have the tactics. You have the mindset. You have the community.

 

[4:42:40] The only thing standing between you and success is action. Implementation. Execution.

 

[4:43:00] So go out there and do it. Close deals. Change lives. Become the trusted advisor that businesses need.

 

[4:43:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Boom! Let's Go!"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Encerramento energético final

 

[4:43:22] Boom! Let's go, inner circle family! Let's go!

 

[4:43:40] I'll see you in the community. I'll see you in the next class. I'll see you in your boom posts.

 

[4:44:00] Thank you, everyone. Thank you, Gael. Thank you, Adam. Thank you, Dana. Thank you to all our guest speakers.

 

[4:44:20] And thank you to all of you for being here. For your commitment. For your dedication. For your desire to become better.

 

[4:44:40] Now go out there and make it happen. Boom!

 

[4:45:00] [END OF CLASS]

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO COMPLETO (CONSOLIDADO)

 

NÚMEROS TOTAIS DA MASTERCLASS:

 

DURAÇÃO E PARTICIPAÇÃO:

6+ horas - Duração total da masterclass

4+ semanas - Duração do programa UHTC até este ponto

128 slides - Total de slides na apresentação

3 AM - Horário de Dana (Bélgica)

5 AM - Horário de Bogdan

5:45 AM - Horário de Nick (Grécia)

2.5 horas - Checkpoint de duração

6-12 páginas - Notas tomadas pelos participantes

50+ screenshots - Alternativa de alguns participantes

 

DEALS E RESULTADOS FINANCEIROS:

$5,000/mês - Deal recorrente de Scott (até 1 ano = $60K+)

$10,000 - Segundo deal de Scott em pipeline

$50,000 - Deal de Pansy (grants governamentais)

$10 million - Mínimo para financial instruments

$50 billion - Deal de SBLC que Pansy trabalhou

$100K-$200K - Deals típicos mencionados

$500 million - Exemplo de fund deal (investment banking)

$500K - Investimento de mentor em empresa de Desmond

$3,000 - Exemplo de budget atual de marketing

$10,000 - ROI projetado (3.5x)

$500-600/mês - Investimento de Desmond em CPA

 

EMPRESAS E VALUATIONS:

$170 billion - Valuation da Salesforce

72 anos - Idade da empresa Vancouver Fire Radio Security

1.5x - Aumento de sales por Connie em 8 anos

40,000+ propriedades - Database da empresa de Nick

100+ localizações - Franquia de academias mencionada

10-12 branches - Empresa de real estate de Nick

 

MÉTRICAS DE VENDAS:

100 leads/mês - Volume SMB

10 leads/mês - Volume Enterprise

70-80% - Taxa de automação SMB

70% - Prospects self-educated

1-3 calls - Para fechar SMB

<30 dias - Ciclo SMB

3-6 meses a 1 ano - Ciclo financial instruments

30%, 60%, 90% dias - Termos de pagamento típicos

3 anos - Perspectiva TCO típica

52% - Fortune companies que desapareceram em 15 anos

 

EXPERIÊNCIAS E BACKGROUNDS:

25 anos - Experiência corporativa de Pansy

12 anos - Negócio próprio de Scott antes HTC

2 anos - Scott na comunidade HTC

12 anos - Desmond no Japão

20 anos - Experiência do champion de Nick

4 meses - Nick para se tornar top SDR

2008-2009 - Período de empresas japonesas com papel

2014-2017 - Período de burnout de Bogdan

12-16 horas/dia - Jornada durante burnout

4.5-6 horas - Sono durante burnout

9 horas - Distância da mudança de cidade

 

ESTRUTURAS E FRAMEWORKS:

5 stakeholders - Níveis de stakeholders (Sage, Creator, Hero, Explorer, Ruler)

5 passos - Storyline de slide deck

8 passos - Processo de procurement

4 elementos - Framework de Adam para SMB

4 níveis - Product ladder de Bogdan

3 níveis - Framework Discover→Clarify→Quantify

6 pessoas - Máximo de gestão efetiva (Extreme Ownership)

4 Gems - Personalidades (Ruby, Emerald, Sapphire, Sage)

 

FRAMEWORKS COMPLETOS:

 

1. Discover → Clarify → Quantify (Scott)

Aplicação:

Recap de dores e objetivos

Discover: Decision criteria, decision process

Clarify: Perguntas básicas para clarificar processo

Quantify: Timeline, stakeholders envolvidos, tempo entre calls

 

Resultado: $5K/mês deal fechado semana seguinte à implementação

 

2. Gael's Product Pyramid (Bogdan)

4 Níveis:

Free: Pro bonos, YouTube, conteúdo gratuito

LCP (Low Cost Product): Associates júnior, webinars, Dan Lok Shop

HTP (High Ticket Product): Senior staff, HTC/HIC, Dragon 100

Back End: Partners, colaborações, deals estratégicos

 

Uso: Avaliar se empresa vale investimento de tempo (precisa ter todos 4)

 

3. Kingpin Strategy (Nick - CIA)

Processo:

Ultimate 100 List (Rich J. - UHDC Class 2)

Identificar small hinge = large door

Mapear organização completa

Identificar champion/sage

Empty cup approach

Credibility lending

Indirect compliment strategy

 

Resultado: Acesso a 40,000 propriedades

 

4. Des Triangle (Nick - UHDC Class 4.1)

Aplicação:

Escolher um lado do triângulo para expandir

Nick escolheu marketing

Demonstrar expertise através de soluções

Build rapport + authority simultaneamente

 

5. 5-Step Slide Deck Storyline

Big Change: Nome mudança na indústria

Winners vs Losers: Desenvolva o vilão

Future Pacing: Pinte o sonho

Solution as Hero: Introduza solução

Evidence: Social proofs, testimonials

 

Exemplos: Zuora (subscription economy), Salesforce (4th industrial revolution)

 

6. Buyer Awareness Stages (Adam)

Stage 1 - Oblivious:

Não sabem que têm problema

Solução: Educar sobre problema

 

Stage 2 - Awareness:

Sabem do problema, não da solução

Solução: Educar sobre soluções

Erro: Pular stages (soa como pitch ou arrogante)

 

Stage 3 - Inertia:

Sabem problema e severidade, mas não agem

Solução: Scarcity, consequências de inação

Técnica: "What happens if you don't change?"

Timing: Só funciona neste stage

 

Stage 4 - Searching:

Superaram inércia, procuram alternativas

Solução: Diferenciar sua oferta

 

Crítico: NUNCA pular stages, personalizar conteúdo

 

7. B2B Procurement Process (8 Steps)

RFP (Request for Proposal)

Se recebe last minute = sendo shopped

Pro Tip: Draft RFP com sales a seu favor

Vendor Registration (VRF)

Acontece steps 3-4 na prática

Legal compliance, due diligence

Purchase Requisition (PR)

Raised by procurement

Blanket PR = annual allocation

Purchase Order (PO)

Legal binding document

PO number = required for payment

Como AE é creditado

Legal T&Cs

Terms and conditions approval

Pro Tip: Be nice to legal

Procurement Approval

Pro Tip: Be nice to procurement

Invoice Submission

Requires PO number

Delivery before payment (B2B)

Finance Payment

AR/AP management

30/60/90 day terms

CFO final sign-off

 

Golden Rule: Quem escreve RFP geralmente ganha deal

 

8. CFO Closing Blueprint

 

CFO Mindset:

Core issue = money

Everything = ROI

Multiple ROIs simultaneously

Limited resources

Competing initiatives (não só competitors)

 

CapEx vs OpEx:

Capitalize: All at once (lower profits at start)

Expense: When expense occurs (lower profits per period)

Quarter Management: 90-day terms = next quarter

 

TCO (Total Cost of Ownership):

3-year perspective comum em tech

Refresh cycle consideration

 

CFO as Friend Strategy:

Internal CFO Lunch:

YOU pay (não expense)

Learn criterias, priorities

Get perspective on client CFO

Finance Team Consultation:

They assess deals

Provide risk assessment

Help navigate client finance

Questions to Ask:

Marketing spend?

Legal spend?

P&L breakdown?

Highest costs? (usually people)

Economic benefits consideration?

 

Value Proposition Examples:

"Only 10% of marketing spend"

"Legal expenses savings"

"Training improves productivity = revenue margins"

"People costs = highest, we reduce that"

 

The Challenge:

Find CFO/CPA/Controller

1-6 months relationship building

Role play with them

Get pragmatic feedback

1-10 scale: Would they approve?

Identify tipping point

 

9. Pansy's Negotiation Framework

 

Wrong Way (No UHTC):

Fold under pressure

Offer discounts immediately

Add deliverables to justify discount

No verbal commitment

No urgency created

 

Right Way (With UHTC):

Understand Real Objection:

"Help me understand how knocking down fees helps you push forward"

Clarify Authority:

Who needs to approve?

What's the process?

Verbal Commitment:

"Does that mean we'll be doing business?"

Get commitment before concession

Create Urgency:

"4th meeting already"

"You've spoken with [sage]"

Conditional Concession:

"IF I do this, THEN will you...?"

 

Result: $50K deal closed

 

10. Financial Instruments Process (Pansy)

 

SBLC (Standby Letter of Credit):

Buyer/Seller contracts

Commodities: crude oil, LNG, jet fuel

$10M minimum to billions

3-6 months to 1 year cycle

 

Key Documents:

NDA: Non-Disclosure Agreement

NCNDA: Non-Convention + Non-Disclosure

Prevents bypassing

Signed at beginning

Fine print CRITICAL (lawsuits possible)

LOI: Letter of Intent

 

Due Diligence:

Verify legitimacy

Match specs

Track records

Testimonials

Prevent scams

 

Jargon: SBLC, DD, LOI, IMF, BF, TCO, AR, AP, CapEx, OpEx

 

External Authorities:

CD Bank

Bank of America

Offshore banks

 

ESTRATÉGIAS DE IMPLEMENTAÇÃO:

 

Small Business Closing (Adam)

Características:

100 leads/mês

1-3 call close

<30 days cycle

70-80% automation

Owner = control freak

B2C mindset in B2B space

Take things personally

Forget quickly (miss 1-2 days = problema)

 

Automation:

Email sequences

Webinars

Information delivery

Only show up to take check

 

Follow-up:

30-day increments

10-12 touchpoints

Not just calls (email, SMS, LinkedIn)

Creative: Call Tuesday, Email Wednesday, LinkedIn Thursday, Donut Friday

Uber Eats coffee delivery

 

Persuasion (Pre-suasion):

Control information (70% self-educated)

Send white papers

Third-party content (não seu)

Shape decision process

Win before fighting

 

Enterprise Closing

Características:

10 leads/mês

Multiple months cycle

More hands-on

Multiple layers, departments, time zones

Structured management

 

Nick's Success Principles

Sun Tzu Application:

Win first, then fight

Map organization

Arm yourself with industry knowledge

Choose battles carefully

 

Intelligence Gathering:

Talk to staff (gatekeepers)

Learn manager personality

Hobbies, sleep patterns

Best times to call

Multiple locations strategy

 

Champion Development:

Find sage/trusted person

Invest them in your success

Empty cup approach

Let them coach you

Credibility lending

Indirect compliments

 

Results:

Top SDR in 4 months

40,000 properties access

LA law firm (remote from Greece)

 

TAG TEAM STRATEGIES:

 

Male/Female Closer Advantages:

Different perspectives

Prevent costly errors

Cover different stages

Secret signals (iPads, notebooks)

Pre-arrangements before meetings

Complementary skills

 

Pansy's Industry:

Male-dominated (financial instruments)

Female advantage in teamwork

High EQ + Business Acumen = cut above rest

 

ASSIGNMENTS COMPLETOS:

 

Assignment 1: Sharpen Business Acumen

Read 3 business magazines (Entrepreneur, Inc., Financial Times, Bloomberg, Barron's, WSJ)

Read 3 companies' public financial statements

Listen to earning calls

Handwrite articles (changes synapses)

Learn vernacular and vocabulary

Avoid being a "pig" (pretender)

 

Assignment 2: Practice Framework

Implement Discover → Clarify → Quantify

Role play with accountability partner

Apply in real deals

Get feedback

 

Assignment 3: Create Slide Deck

Follow 5-step storyline

Big change → Winners/Losers → Future pacing → Solution → Evidence

Not marketing deck, pitch closing deck

May take 1-3 months

 

Assignment 4: Map Procurement

8 steps for your client/company

Understand current position

Know what's coming next

Start early on vendor registration

 

Assignment 5: Role Play & Record

Record role plays

Watch back critically

What went well?

What to improve?

Get feedback

 

Bonus Assignment: Industry Expert

Read trade publications

Understand challenges

Know jargon

Know trends

Know major players

Know what's changing

 

PAIN POINTS IDENTIFICADOS:

 

CFO Challenges:

Multiple ROIs to manage

Limited resources

Competing initiatives

Cash flow management

Quarter-end pressures

Risk management

Approval authority limits

Balancing short-term vs long-term

 

Small Business Owner:

Multiple hats

No managers (>6 people = chaos)

Staffing difficulties

No time to coach salespeople

Take business personally

B2C mindset in B2B

Forget quickly

Want CEO treatment but act emotional

 

AE/Closer Challenges:

Understanding finance

Speaking CFO language

Long procurement cycles

Multiple stakeholder alignment

Detail-oriented documentation

Payment term negotiations

Getting past gatekeepers

Managing 100 leads/month

 

Enterprise Challenges:

Multiple decision makers

Different time zones

Complex approval processes

Long sales cycles

Political dynamics

Budget cycles

 

SOLUÇÕES OFERECIDAS:

 

For Overwhelm:

Find ONE thing

Implement immediately

Don't try to master everything

One foot in front of the other

Unconscious competence (like driving)

Consistent action over perfection

 

For Lack of Experience:

Start with assignments

Role play extensively

Study frameworks

Learn industry

Leverage community

Start with SMB, level up to enterprise

 

For Rejection:

Not personal, it's business

Ask for feedback

Learn from it

Move on

Plenty of opportunities

 

For Time Management:

Systems and automation

CRM usage

Email sequences

Templates

Prioritize leads

30-day increments

Schedule touchpoints

 

For Pricing Objections:

Establish value first

Price = proxy for other concerns

Don't fold immediately (Pansy)

Ask questions

Tie back to ROI

Show cost of inaction

 

MINDSET E FILOSOFIA COMPLETA:

 

Core Principles:

"I am a trusted advisor"

"Want the deal but don't need the deal"

"Focus on the moon, not your finger" (Bruce Lee)

"How you do anything is how you do everything"

"Talk less, make results"

"Empty your cup"

"Win first, then fight" (Sun Tzu)

"Don't be a pig" (financial industry)

"Procurement is my friend"

"CFO is your friend"

"Never late, always soon" (Desmond)

 

Journey Mindset:

Jackrabbit, tortoise, or sloth (doesn't matter)

One foot in front of the other

One day at a time

Consistent action

Don't stop

Better version than weeks ago

Unconscious competence level

 

Implementation Over Information:

Information without implementation = entertainment

Find ONE thing

Implement THIS WEEK

Don't wait

Take action NOW

Your brothers/sisters are doing it

If they can, so can you

 

Community Philosophy:

When you win, we all win

Post your wins

Inspire others

Pay it forward (after mastering)

Abundance within community

Support each other

Role play together

Accountability partners

CASE STUDIES COMPLETOS:

 

Scott:

Background: 2 anos HTC, 12 anos business owner, B2C→B2B

Challenge: Rambling, no clarity em decision criteria/process

Solution: Discover→Clarify→Quantify framework

Result: $5K/mês recurring (até 1 ano), $10K deal em pipeline

Key: Focused on ONE framework, adapted to his way

 

Nick:

Background: Season 10 HTC, legal admin→SDR→top performer→LA law firm

Challenge: Green monster environment, gatekeepers mockery

Solution: Kingpin strategy, map organization, champion development

Result: 40,000 properties access, remote work from Greece

Key: Sun Tzu strategy, intelligence gathering, empty cup

 

Bogdan:

Background: Civil engineer, 2008 crisis, 12-16h days, burnout, debt

Challenge: Information overload, noise in head, couldn't focus

Solution: Leverage knowledge vs accumulate, connect dots, Gael's pyramid

Result: 6-figure+ deal potential, NDA on bigger deal

Key: Changed environment (9h away), focused on principles

 

Pansy:

Background: 25 anos MNC, Season 9 HTC, mom, grants specialist

Challenge: Budget negotiations, no verbal commitment

Solution: UHTC negotiation framework, work with heroes

Result: $50K grant deal, financial instruments ($10M-$50B)

Key: High EQ, tag team strategy, NCNDA expertise

 

EMPRESAS E EXEMPLOS:

 

Technology:

Salesforce ($170B) - CRM, cloud, customer relationship

HubSpot - Marketing automation, went IPO

Zendesk - Customer service, went IPO

Box - Cloud storage, went IPO

Oracle - Legacy software (villain in Salesforce story)

IBM - Legacy software (villain in Salesforce story)

 

Digital Marketing:

Zuora - Subscription economy platform

Digital Hive - 3rd largest agency North America

 

Financial:

CD Bank - Offshore banking

Bank of America - Financial instruments authority

Bloomberg - Financial news, subscription targeting "pigs"

 

Other:

Amazon - Day One philosophy, customer obsession

Vancouver Fire Radio Security - 72 years, 1.5x growth under Connie

Dan Lok Enterprise - Multiple programs, client obsession

 

LOCALIDADES E CULTURAS:

 

Time Zones Represented:

Belgium (Dana - 3 AM)

Greece (Nick - 5:45 AM)

Singapore (Pansy)

Europe/Africa/Central Asia (early mornings)

Asia (early mornings)

North America (various)

 

Cultural Insights:

Japan (2008-2009): Pen and paper, shared computer, Rolodex

Singapore: Can meet (max 5 people)

Financial Industry: Male-dominated, especially instruments

B2B: Relationship-centric (vs transactional)

 

PRÓXIMOS PASSOS E CHAMADAS PARA AÇÃO:

 

Immediate (Tonight):

Write down ONE thing to implement

Put on calendar

Make non-negotiable

Commit to execution

 

This Week:

Implement chosen framework

Role play with accountability partner

Start reading business magazine

Begin studying one company's financials

 

This Month:

Complete all 5 assignments

Find and connect with CFO/CPA

Create slide deck (if applicable)

Map procurement process

Post wins in community

 

Long Term (1-6 months):

Build CFO relationship

Role play with finance professional

Become industry expert

Level up from SMB to Enterprise

Close first major deal

Post boom in community

Support Available:

Facebook group (post wins, questions)

Accountability groups

Brothers and sisters support

FINAL STATISTICS:

 

Content Delivered:

6+ hours live training

128 slides

4 major role plays

4 case studies (Scott, Nick, Bogdan, Pansy)

10+ frameworks

5 assignments + 1 bonus

Q&A session

Multiple guest speakers

 

Value Propositions Demonstrated:

$5K→$60K+ (Scott's recurring)

$10K pipeline (Scott)

$50K grants (Pansy)

$10M-$50B financial instruments (Pansy)

40,000 properties (Nick)

6-figure+ potential (Bogdan)

 

Implementation Rate Expected:

Find ONE thing: 100%

Complete assignments: 30-40%

Role play consistently: 20-30%

Close first deal: 10-20%

Become CFO expert: 5-10%

Master all frameworks: 1-5%

 

Success Formula: Knowledge + Frameworks + Implementation + Consistency + Community = Ultra High Ticket Closer

 

CRITICAL SUCCESS FACTORS:

 

Mindset: Trusted advisor, not salesperson

Knowledge: Business acumen, industry expertise

Skills: Frameworks, processes, techniques

Relationships: Stakeholders, CFO, internal team

Systems: CRM, automation, follow-up

Persistence: One foot in front of other

Community: Support, accountability, inspiration

Implementation: Action over information

Adaptation: Learn from feedback, iterate

Patience: Long cycles, relationship building

 

 

ENCERRAMENTO:

 

Esta masterclass de 6+ horas representa possivelmente o treinamento mais completo já oferecido sobre Ultra High Ticket B2B Closing, cobrindo desde fundamentos de mindset até táticas avançadas de CFO closing, com exemplos reais variando de $5K a $50 bilhões em deals.

 

Mensagem Final de Desmond: "Information without implementation is just entertainment. You have everything you need to succeed. The only thing missing is action. Go out there and close some deals. Boom!"

 

Call to Action Final: Before bed tonight: Write down ONE thing to implement this week. Schedule it. Make it non-negotiable. Execute.

 

Community Promise: "When you win, we all win. Post your booms. Inspire your brothers and sisters. Become the trusted advisor that businesses need."

 

Instructor Sign-off: "Never late, always soon. This is Desmond Soon, signing off. Go out there and close some deals. Boom! Let's go!"

 

DURAÇÃO TOTAL: 6+ horas FORMATO: Masterclass interativa ao vivo com role plays, Q&A, e implementação prática REPLAY: Disponível para revisão e estudo PRÓXIMA AULA: A ser anunciada COMUNIDADE: Inner Circle Family - HTC/UHTC Program

 

VALOR ENTREGUE: Incalculável - frameworks que podem gerar deals de 6, 7, 8 figures ao longo da carreira.
